chapters.summary: Council staff welcomes attendees to the Transportation and Infrastructure Committee hearing, asks everyone to silence phones, and instructs those wishing to speak to fill out appearance cards with the sergeants.
chapters.title: Council staff gives hearing housekeeping instructions

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Welcome to today's New York City Council hearing for the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, please silence all phones and electronic devices.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you wish to speak in today's hearing, please fill out an appearance card with one of the sergeants.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Moving forward, no one is to approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair, you may begin.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Shaun Abreu opens the hearing and introduces the five pieces of legislation: Int 845 on lighting under elevated trains, Int 866 on disruptive work signage, Int 894 on seasonal sidewalk cafe enclosures, Int 917 on cafe cleanliness, and Int 918 on quarterly fee payments.
chapters.title: Chair Abreu opens hearing and introduces five bills

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to today's hearing of the New York City Council's Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Shaun-Abreu, Majority Leader and Chair of this committee.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Today the committee will hear testimony on five pieces of legislation.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Introduction number 845, sponsored by Councilmember Thomas Henry, in relation to requiring the Department of Transportation to install lighting under elevated train lines.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Introduction number 866, sponsored by Councilmember Epstein, in relation to requiring the Department of Transportation to post notification signs regarding upcoming disruptive work.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Introduction number 894, sponsored by Councilmember Brewer, in relation to seasonal enclosures for sidewalk cafes.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Introduction number 917, sponsored by Councilmember Justin Sanchez, in relation to sanitation and cleanliness requirements for roadway and sidewalk cafes.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And introduction number 918, also sponsored by Councilmember Thomas Henry, in relation to allowing sidewalk and roadway cafe revocable consent fees to be paid in...

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: quarterly installments.

chapters.summary: Abreu explains the hearing will examine the purpose, implementation, and impact of the bills, and that he looks forward to testimony from the administration, restaurant owners, community members, and advocates on how the proposals affect neighborhoods.
chapters.title: Chair Abreu on hearing purpose and stakeholder input

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Because today's hearing is focused solely on legislation, we will use it as an opportunity to examine the purpose, implementation, and potential impact of these bills.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Together, these bills raise important questions about how New York City manages its streets, sidewalks, and public spaces.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Whether we are discussing lighting beneath elevated train lines, public notice requirements for work on our streets,

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: or the cleanliness, affordability, or seasonal availability of outdoor dining, the issue at the heart of these bills is how to make our public spaces safer, more accessible, and better maintained for everyone.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I look forward to hearing from the administration about their views on these bills, including any operational or fiscal concerns, as well as any recommendations on how these bills could be improved.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I also look forward to hearing from restaurant owners, community members, advocates, and other stakeholders about how these proposals would affect them in the neighborhoods they serve.

chapters.summary: Abreu thanks committee staff including Jaleesa Quigley, Theodore Miller, Sierra Townsend, Kevin Katowski, John Bazile, Sarah Swain, Natalie Meltzer, and gives a special farewell to principal financial analyst Adrian Jipal.
chapters.title: Chair Abreu thanks committee staff

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Before I hand it over to my colleagues to speak on their bills, I would like to thank my staff and the committee staff for their hard work in preparing for today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Jaleesa Quigley, my Deputy Chief of Staff, Theodore Miller and Sierra Townsend, Councils to the Committee, Kevin Katowski and John Bazile, Senior Policy Analysts of the Committee, and Sarah Swain and Nat.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Natalie Meltzer, staff to the Committee on Consumer Worker Protection, who have been instrumental in developing the Council's outdoor dining legislation.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I'd also like to give a special thanks to Adrian Jipal, who has been the principal financial analyst of the committee for

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: several years for his commitment, savvy, and unfailing good humor in working to better this committee, the council, and our city.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Adrian, thank you for your service and good luck in your next endeavor.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: We miss your bow ties.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I will now pass it over to my colleagues to speak on their bills, starting with Councilmember

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Gail Brewer.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Gale Brewer explains her bill allowing restaurants to install high-quality winter vestibules enclosing licensed sidewalk cafe areas from October 1 to March 31, inspired by the Hospitality Alliance, to help businesses utilize year-round paid space during cold months.
chapters.title: Councilmember Brewer presents Intro 894 on seasonal sidewalk cafe enclosures

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I am here to talk about, as you heard from the chair, 894.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It has to do with seasonal enclosures for cafes that are outdoor.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And this did come from the suggestion made by the Hospitality Alliance.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I want to thank them, as I always do, for their incredible support of these kinds of issues.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: There's a time frame during the season when it's chilly, as you all know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And the idea would be because of that frigid and wet weather across our city, especially between October 1st and March 31, during that time period,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Intro 894, if enacted into law, would allow restaurants to install high-quality winter vestibules enclosing their licensed sidewalk cafe area.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: These are spaces for which they already pay the city for the year, actually for the whole year.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so the notion would be to have better access to that space.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: There are also businesses that are unclear on what is allowed and what is prohibited.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This legislation aims to provide clear guidance to help businesses and owners plan.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think we all see, in some cases, businesses have these enclosures, sometimes they don't, and they all differ.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This bill would make it clear.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I do want to thank Andrew Reggie from the New York City Hospitality Alliance for raising this issue.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And for his tireless advocacy for restaurants and bars and cafes and owners and people who are crucial to our city's economy and neighborhood life.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I want to thank Chair Rehu for including this on the agenda and legislative staff, Rachel Cadoro, Sarah Swain, Natalie Meltzer, as well as committee staff, Sierra Townsend, Theo Miller, John Basile, and Kevin Kutowski.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much, and we hope that it will pass.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I will now turn it over to Councilmember Sanchez to speak on Intro 917.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Justin Sanchez briefly introduces his bill on sanitation and cleanliness requirements for roadway and sidewalk cafes, thanking the chair and committee staff and expressing excitement about improving cleanliness standards.
chapters.title: Councilmember Sanchez introduces Intro 917 on cafe cleanliness standards

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Intro 917, one, first and foremost, I want to thank the chair and all of the committee staff for having this on the agenda today.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: We're really excited to hear more about how this bill can help actually improve cleanliness standards across the city as we roll out outdoor dining.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And we look forward to hearing a substantive question and answer period.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

chapters.summary: Abreu reads Epstein's statement on Intro 866, which would require posted signs within a 500-foot radius of planned disruptive work at least 48 hours in advance, including contact information, to increase transparency and accessibility for residents, schools, and businesses affected by construction.
chapters.title: Chair Abreu reads Councilmember Epstein's remarks on Intro 866 disruptive work signage

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I'll now read Councilmember Epstein's remarks on introduction number A66.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you to Chair Abreu and all my colleagues for the opportunity to hear my bill, Intro 866, at today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: My office has already had discussions with DOT and will be moving forward with amending this bill to require the entity obtaining the permit, not a DOT, to put up the signage.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: We all know the feeling.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: You wake up to unexpected noise coming from the sidewalk below.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: You're walking your child to school and suddenly the path you take every day is taped off for construction.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: You go to open up your deli for the day, only to find someone jackhammering outside of your business.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: You didn't know this was going to happen, and you're not sure how long it will last.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: This type of disruptive work presents additional challenges for individuals who use assistive mobility devices who may struggle to get around if their typical sidewalk route is changed.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: While the work being done to improve our streets and sidewalks

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: walks is necessary, it can be disruptive to our daily life.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: It is critical that all New Yorkers have access to the information necessary so they can plan ahead for when construction projects arise.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: My bill, Intro 866, addresses this all too common occurrence by providing New Yorkers with posted signs within a 500 foot radius of the location of planned disruptive work at least 48 hours before the work is scheduled to begin.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: It would also include contact information, ensuring the public knows who to talk to about the work if there are questions or concerns.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Constructions can be disruptive to surrounding residents, schools, and businesses.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: It is important that neighbors have advance notice of disruptive construction so they can make necessary plans.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: This bill is about increasing transparency between city agencies and residents and increasing accessibility in our government system.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I look forward to hearing feedback about this bill, about how we can make our streets more accessible for everyone.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: That was a statement by my colleague, Councilmember Epstein.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And I would like to recognize the colleagues who have joined us today, Council Members Brewer, Wong, Krishnan, Justin Sanchez-Lewis, and Thomas Henry.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I will now pass it over to my colleague, Council Member Thomas Henry, to speak on her bill.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Shanel Thomas-Henry presents Intro 845 requiring DOT to assess and install lighting under elevated train lines, citing safety concerns along Roosevelt Avenue under the 7 train, and Intro 918 allowing roadway cafe operators to pay revocable consent fees in quarterly installments to ease financial burdens on small businesses.
chapters.title: Councilmember Thomas-Henry presents Intros 845 and 918

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Just need one second.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So good morning, Chair Brehu, and thank you for convening today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you to my colleagues on the committee.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Transportation is about more than getting people from one place to another.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: It is about how people feel our neighborhoods, people experience our neighborhoods once they arrive.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: It's about whether a resident feels safe walking home beneath an elevated train.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: It's about whether a family chooses to stop by at a neighborhood cafe.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: It's about whether our streets invite people in or push them away.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Today, as a member of this committee,

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: As a member of this committee and the chair of Small Business Committee, I am proud to present two pieces of legislation that recognize a simple truth.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Our transportation system should work for the people who use it every day.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Introduction 845 would require the Department of Transportation to assess streets and sidewalks beneath elevated train infrastructure to determine where additional lighting is needed and install it within a defined time frame.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: In my district, the 7 train runs

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: through the heart of our community.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: For years, residents have shared concerns about darkness, dark under the underpasses that feel unsafe and overlooked.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: No New Yorker should have to question their safety simply because they are walking home beneath our transit infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: This legislation creates accountability by ensuring those concerns are evaluated, addressed, and reported on.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Introduction 918 focuses on another important part of our streetscape, the small businesses that activate our sidewalks and make our commercial corridors vibrant.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: By allowing roadway cafe operators to pay revocable consent fees in quarterly installments rather than all at once,

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: we can ease unnecessary financial burdens while continuing to support the outdoor dining program that has become such an important part of our city's fabric.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: These bills may address different issues, but they advance the same goal, creating streets that are safe and more economically vibrant.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: That is the kind of transportation system we should continue building, one that moves people while also strengthening the communities they call home.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you, and I look forward to today's discussion.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I'd like to add that we've been joined by Councilmember Hanif on Zoom and Councilmember Denowitz.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, and I'll turn it over to testimony from the administration.

chapters.summary: Council staff swears in the administration witnesses before they begin their testimony.
chapters.title: Administration witnesses sworn in

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please raise your right hand.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin when you're ready.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And if you could just state your name and record for name and title for the record before you begin, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Deputy Commissioner Monty Dean explains DOT's lighting standards, installation methods, and challenges with lighting under elevated trains including shadows, structural limitations, and vibration. He expresses concern that the bill's requirement for distinctive lighting would slow implementation and offers to discuss enhancing lighting in specific districts.
chapters.title: DOT testimony on Intro 845: lighting under elevated train lines

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair Abreu, members of the Transportation Committee, of the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: My name is Monty Dean, Deputy Commissioner of Traffic Operations.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: I am joined by Michelle Craven, Associate Deputy Commissioner of Cityscape and Franchises.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Jeff Malamy, Director of Street Improvement Projects, and Rick Rodriguez, Assistant Commissioner for Intergovernmental and Community Affairs.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify on behalf of Mayor Mondani and Commissioner Flynn about Intro 845, 866, 894, 917, and 918.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: First, Intro 845 is sponsored by Councilmember Thomas Henry.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: This bill would require DOT to install distinctive lighting under elevated train lines.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: With nearly 400,000 streetlights citywide under our responsibility, we recognize the importance of street lighting and enhancing both street

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: safety, and quality of life.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We are committed to ensuring streets are well lit for vehicle traffic as well as pedestrians in both the roadway and the sidewalk.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: As a national leader in sustainable street lighting, our lighting studies are based on DOT standards.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: These standards include recommended practices and guidelines published by the Illuminating Engineering Society, the internationally recognized entity that sets recommended practices for lighting levels in various applications to ensure lighting standards are sufficiently addressed citywide.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We are happy to provide a quick breakdown of how lighting is installed to provide more background.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Based on study evaluations, we routinely make upgrades in any street reconstruction project.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: For installation, there are three ways we can add lighting, depending on where the lower lighting levels are located.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We can reduce the distance between streetlights.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Add secondary lighting on the back of existing poles over the sidewalk at a lower height, or install new standalone poles.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: DOT must consider various factors when installing lighting, including extensive coordination with contractors and utilities when working around obstructions caused by trees or hydrants and existing below-ground infrastructure like gas mains and electric ducts.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: These factors require further extensive survey and design work.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Installations can also require time-consuming and disruptive street excavations to lay new conduits and cables, as well as conduit construction for new feeds.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Our team works every day to make sure our streets and roadways are well lit.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: 25 alone, we enhanced lighting at nearly 2,500 locations across the city.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Despite these efforts around the city, we hear from New Yorkers that they feel safer and more secure when streets and sidewalks under elevated train lines are more brightly lit.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Lighting under elevated trains is uniquely challenging because the support columns create shadows and the elevated structures limit the height of the street light poles, and the elevated structures are not engineered to support additional loads.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Further, high vibration levels can prevent the installation of DOT equipment.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Along with the installation process, DOT has two categories of lighting we typically install, standard and distinctive.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Any streetlight pole other than the standard steel octagonal and davit designs for city streets is considered distinctive.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: There are seven types of distinctive streetlights, which are installed as part of streetscape projects and other elected funded initiatives requiring public design commission approval.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Due to the different funding streams and additional approval processes, distinctive lighting is generally more expensive and slower to implement.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: While DOT strives to be responsive in addressing community concerns regarding sufficient lighting, we have concerns with the bill as written.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We are worried that this bill would have the unintended consequences of slowing down our ability to address lighting conditions by limiting us to distinctive lighting.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We would be happy to discuss with the council how we can enhance lighting in your districts and welcome feedback if there are particular locations of concern.

chapters.summary: Dean states that DOT already posts informational signs for long-term construction projects and uses street ambassadors and variable message signs. He expresses concerns that the bill's broad scope would slow construction and raise costs, and prefers utilizing existing notification requirements.
chapters.title: DOT testimony on Intro 866: disruptive work signage

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Next, Intro 866, sponsored by Councilmember Epstein.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: This bill would require DOT to post signs in advance with

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: in a 500-foot radius of a location of disruptive work.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Disruptive work, as defined in the legislation, means work that is performed by DOT or pursuant to a permit issued by DOT that is expected to create street, sidewalk, or parking space closures,

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: reroute traffic, relocation of bus stops or bike share stations, or work between 8 p.m.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: And 8 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: That includes noise, vibrations, or bright lighting.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: DOT already posts informational signs for long-term construction projects pursuant to existing requirements.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We understand the need to communicate effectively to New Yorkers when there will be disruptive construction work.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We believe that existing rules and current practices accomplish the goals of this bill while balancing operational needs.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: In addition to the informational construction signs, we have other tools in our toolkit to appropriately notify and involve communities within project areas.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: For example, our street ambassadors meet people where they are by discussing project impacts in communities on the street.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We also deploy variable message signs when needed.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Of course, we also are proud of the ongoing partnerships we have with the council and the community boards to utilize your networks to spread the word with residents about critical projects in your neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Although we support the goal of the bill as described with our existing toolkit of communication efforts, we have a number of concerns with the current scope of the bill and would prefer to utilize existing requirements on construction signage to meet the intended goals.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: With affordability and project delivery a top priority in this administration, we would be wary of creating additional requirements which would slow

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: instruction raised costs.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We'd be happy to continue conversations with the council to identify the best way to implement notice requirements.

chapters.summary: Dean expresses support for the goals of Intro 894, noting sidewalk cafes can already use overhead coverings. He suggests including other winterization methods like vertical screenings and aligning timelines with ongoing discussions about roadway cafe winterization.
chapters.title: DOT testimony on Intro 894: seasonal enclosures for sidewalk cafes

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Next, intro 894 sponsored by Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: This bill would allow sidewalk cafes to include removable vertical screenings and overhead coverings from October 1st to March 31st.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We are thrilled with the significant interest in expanding the Dining Out NYC program, and we support the goals of this bill, as sidewalk cafes are already allowed to operate with overhead coverings and awnings pursuant to our rules, along with our sister agency's requirements in specific instances.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We have suggestions for how to include other winterization methods, like vertical screenings,

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: and may want to modify the timeline so the requirements are in alignment with existing discussions on winterization for roadway cafes.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We are happy to have more conversations with the council on how best to enhance the sidewalk cafe experience.

chapters.summary: Dean explains the bill would codify existing DOT rules on cleanliness and raise maximum civil penalties to $300 for first violations and $600 for subsequent ones. He emphasizes the Dining Out NYC program's existing cleaning requirements and suggests treating all program violations uniformly.
chapters.title: DOT testimony on Intro 917: cleanliness standards for cafes

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Next, Intro 917, sponsored by Councilmember Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: This bill would codify existing DOT rules pertaining to cleanliness standards for businesses operating sidewalk and roadway cafes.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: The bill would also raise maximum civil penalties to $300 for the first violation and $600 for second and subsequent violations at the same place of business.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Prioritizing quality of life concerns is critical to the success of our Dining Out NYC program.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We have been working to be incredibly responsive to complaints and carry out enforcement when necessary.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: The Dining Out NYC program requires durable, modular, and easy-to-clean materials for outdoor dining setups.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: establishes weekly cleaning requirements, and prohibits messy sand-filled barriers, which result in a dramatic improvement to the outdoor dining experience.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Regarding the fine structure, we understand the desire to raise the fees, but believe it is important that all program violations related to noncompliance be treated uniformly.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We look forward to working with the council.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: To determine appropriate violation amounts.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We are always striving to make the Dining Out NYC program the best it can be for New Yorkers and visitors, and we are happy to work with the council on this bill to strengthen cleanliness and sanitation standards.

chapters.summary: Dean explains DOT currently requires one-time upfront revocable consent payments and worries quarterly installments would create collection difficulties, staffing burdens, and application delays. He notes DOT charges less than DCWP did, making quarterly payments less relevant, but is open to further discussions.
chapters.title: DOT testimony on Intro 918: quarterly fee payments for cafes

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Finally, in show 918, sponsored by Councilmember Thomas Henry.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: This bill would give businesses the option to pay revocable consent fees in quarterly installments.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Along with addressing quality of life concerns, we are also working every day to help small businesses thrive through outdoor dining, a program that New Yorkers love and that restaurants depend on.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We strive to make participation as accessible and streamlined as possible.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Currently, restaurants must pay an initial revocable consent fee once their application is approved.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Across the board for DOT programs, we require a one-time upfront payment for all revocable consents.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Additionally, it has been DOT's experience that restaurants have a harder time meeting program requirements once they have been authorized to commence operations.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We worry that if this new payment method was authorized, DOT would struggle to collect after the initial payment.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: This would not only leave DOT with a program deficit, but would result in new costs for the department and delays in application approval for the restaurant.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: due to already limited staff capacity being shifted to chase down payments four times a year.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: While quarterly payments perhaps were necessary in the DCWP program when fees were notably higher, DOT charges far less for the revocable consent, making a quarterly payment less relevant.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We share Council's goal to improve the application process and are happy to have more discussions with Council and stakeholders about adapting our program processes.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: In conclusion, I'd like to thank the Council for the opportunity to testify before you today.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We would now be happy to answer any questions.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I'd like to acknowledge we've been joined by Councilmember Pierina Sanchez on Zoom, and I will turn it over to Councilmember Thomas Henry for the first set of questions.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks what criteria DOT uses to decide between standard and distinctive lighting upgrades. Dean explains distinctive lighting is typically funded through elected official allocations while standard lighting uses normal city funding, and that lighting under elevated trains is addressed on a complaint-driven basis.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions DOT on standard vs distinctive lighting criteria

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So first, I'll start with Intro 845.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: What are DOT's current practices for improving street lighting?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Or more specifically, what criteria do you use to decide if a particular corridor will get standard versus distinctive lighting upgrades?

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Distinctive lighting typically comes when we get funded through reso or multimodal funding from an elected official,

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: and that is what is used to purchase and install the distinctive types of lighting, whereas our standard lighting is funded through the normal city funded process.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And what are your current practices regarding lighting conditions on streets?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: streets and sidewalks located underneath elevated subway lines?

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: When we get a request or a complaint, we will go and evaluate conditions at that area, and we'll do data collection, we'll do an assessment of the lighting levels, and then if we determine that there is

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: additional lighting necessary.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We will evaluate what the right approach is, whether it means adding arms onto existing poles or whether we need to install new poles to achieve the right lighting level.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So currently it's not a practice to elevate the lighting levels under elevated subways.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: It's only

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: on an as complaint basis?

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: It's mostly driven by when we get requests or complaints about conditions there.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry raises concerns about darkness and illicit activity under the 7 train on Roosevelt Avenue in her district, noting DOT's lighting improvements have been inconsistent. Dean acknowledges adding arms to existing poles in one section and offers to follow up on additional areas. Thomas-Henry emphasizes the bill aims to help communities that suffer in silence.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions DOT on Roosevelt Avenue 7 train corridor lighting

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Do you have any complaints?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I'll just take a step back.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: The nexus, this bill really comes specifically from conditions, particularly in my district, where the seven train lies in Roosevelt Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: We've had a number, a series of things I've heard you've heard on the news of illicit activity that happens.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And a lot of it is attributed because it's a very dark and desolate area.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I know DOT has attempted some type of lighting.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: It hasn't been consistent.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Do you know in particular about this corridor, what that assessment was?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: There is one section, as you said, where we did come in and we added arms on the back of existing poles.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: If there are additional areas, we can certainly look at adding in other places.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We can check where we've evaluated and what our determination was.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We'd be happy to follow up on that.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And my concern is, I mean, often our communities suffer in silence.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I mean, the advocating usually comes from one or two people, but meanwhile you have an entire community that's affected.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So part of this bill, too, is really to assess those areas where you may have individuals who are not as vocal in advocating for themselves to make sure that they are safe in our city.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks what interagency coordination would be needed to implement the bill. Dean explains DOT may need to coordinate with Con Edison for power, manage trenching and construction, and work with the MTA when work is near transit infrastructure columns.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions DOT on interagency coordination for Intro 845

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Next question as it pertains to 845.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: What interagency coordination would be required to implement this bill?

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We, again, it depends in each location what we find.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: If we find there's a need for additional lighting,

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: If we can't, for instance, add to existing onto existing poles, we might need to work with Con Ed if there needs to be more power provided.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: And then we would have to coordinate on construction if there's additional trenching required.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We'd have to go through the normal process to make sure we know what else is under under the street there.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: And also we would have to.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: If any of the trenching or other work was near any of the transit infrastructure, the columns for the overhead infrastructure, we would have to coordinate closely with them.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: MTA on that as well.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry references DOT's $331 million commitment to replace first-generation LED streetlight fixtures and asks whether it covers lighting under elevated trains. Dean confirms it does for existing fixtures but says additional fixtures would require separate surveys, and the program will take a few years to complete borough by borough.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions DOT on LED replacement program and funding for elevated train areas

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Now, at the committee's executive budget hearing on May 29th of this year, DOT testified to its $331 million commitment to replace every first-generation LED streetlight fixture across the city.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Does that commitment include sufficient funds to replace the lighting fixtures targeted by this bill?

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: It would include the lighting that we have in these areas, yes.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: So we would replace all the existing LED lights, including under transit infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: How long do you anticipate that taking?

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We're working on the timeline now.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We typically have gone by borough, so we'll have to figure out the scheduling of the contract.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: But it will take a few years.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: But the benefit of this program, in addition to replacing existing LEDs,

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: is that we also include installing sensors in the lights so that we will know right away when there are issues with outages or anything like that that will help us identify what the underlying issue is when there are problems like that.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: So we will be able to be more responsive in the future when there are issues with lighting.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: In addition to replacing those LEDs on existing fixtures, would you also look to assess if additional fixtures are needed in those areas?

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We wouldn't necessarily as part of this.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We would have to do separate surveys for areas where there were concerns.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: About the lighting level.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks how often DOT hears concerns about lighting under elevated train lines. Dean says he does not have a specific number but will check and follow up.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions DOT on frequency of lighting complaints under elevated trains

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: How often does DOT hear concerns regarding lighting conditions under elevated train lines?

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: I don't know if we have a specific number.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We can check on that and get back to you on how frequently we get those requests.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I'm going to go to intro 918 for now.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I can come back.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Yeah, I'll come back to you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I think Councilmember Sanchez wants to add commentary on Intro 845.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Sanchez asks about installing lights on the elevated rail itself rather than street poles, citing examples from cities worldwide. Dean explains vibration issues, the trains' own power systems, and coordination challenges with NYC Transit make this atypical, but says he would check if it has been studied.
chapters.title: Sanchez questions DOT on lighting options mounted on elevated rail structures

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Yes, I do.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So if you look around the world, if you go to Santiago, Santo Domingo, if you go to Puerto Rico, Chicago, Bangkok, Kuala Lumpur, Miami,

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: The list goes on.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Seoul, Dubai.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: All of these places around the world have lights specifically on their train rail system.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I'm curious to know if there is an opportunity that doesn't include lighting on actual the street light poles, but on the elevated rail itself.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And what is preventing anyone from adding lights to that elevated rail itself?

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We typically don't because there are vibration issues.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: There's also they have their own power on the train lines that we'd have to contend with.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: So there may be instances where we've done it, but it's not the typical practice.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Oh, it can be challenging.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: It's, you know, for us, it's typically easier because we own and operate the streets and we have usually have some form of existing light poles there that we can add on to.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: So if, for instance, adding a pole on the back of an existing light pole is adding an arm on an existing light pole is an option that that's typically easier to do.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: So is it accurate to say that the street lighting on rails would be more of a state issue than it is a city issue because of the control of the elevated subways?

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We would certainly have to work with them in those instances because they do own the infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: So in those scenarios, we'd have to coordinate definitely with New York City Transit.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Has there been any assessment of using the power that already exists on the trains to illuminate the underneath?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I mean, the way that I envision Councilmember Henry's, Thomas Henry's bill is, I mean, we have lights that you can literally just stick onto a wall with batteries.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Then that was when I was a kid.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And so seeing the way that technology has.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Vastly improved now.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: I'm curious to know if there are green ways and

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: green infrastructure ways that we can be adding light that uses the renewable electricity that already exists on these train tracks to help illuminate underneath.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Is there been any ability to study that or any discussions with either state partners or other city partners about doing something in that way?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Have you been able to envision this legislation in that way at all?

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: I'm not aware that that has been studied, so we'd have to check and see if that's something that's been looked at before.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: All right.

chapters.summary: Sanchez asks about the 1.5-foot cleanliness radius and how it works. Michelle Craven explains sanitation enforces that radius while DOT enforces other cleanliness rules, and reports very few complaints under the new permanent program. Abreu asks about enforcement changes and costs; Craven says the bill would duplicate existing rules with no significant change.
chapters.title: Sanchez and Abreu question DOT on Intro 917 cleanliness enforcement and costs

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I'm now going to, Councilmember Sanchez, if you could just ask your 917 questions now.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Hold on two seconds.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: In terms of 917, so the DOT rule currently have the outdoor calf raider operators keeping their area, I think it's 1.5, the area with a 1.5 foot radius.

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: How is that radius actually working?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: Does that work?

utterances.speaker: Justin E. Sanchez
utterances.text: And how was that 1.5 foot radius come to?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So I believe the 1.5 radius is sanitation's jurisdiction, so they actually enforce that piece of it and we enforce the rest of it.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I will say we've gotten very few complaints about cleanliness in this new permanent outdoor dining program.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: In the emergency program, we had many, many, many complaints about cleanliness.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It's something we've taken very seriously in setting this program up and setting up our rules.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And it's been...

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We've hardly heard about it at all.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And our inspectors do go out and enforce for cleanliness.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We look for waste and debris and graffiti and food scraps and whatever else.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: That's mostly inside the setups, but we've had very few issues.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And how would DOT's approach to enforcement change at all, if at all, with this new legislation?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I think it would be substantially the same.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I mean, we have these rules already, so it wouldn't actually change the requirement.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It would just kind of duplicate it in the ad code, I think.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So we would still enforce it the same way.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And generally, what is the most common cleanliness issue that is reported when you do get the few reports?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Okay, if someone dumps garbage or debris or something outside of the setup.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It's the most common complaint.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And how much does DOT anticipate it would cost to implement 917?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I don't think it would really change things because, again, we already have these in the rules, so it would be the same requirements.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Sounds like a great bill.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: On my time here is done.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Phil Wong raises concerns about inadequate illumination under CSX and Long Island Railroad trestles in his district and asks if DOT can improve lighting there. Dean says DOT can evaluate lighting on its right-of-way and encourages Wong to share specific locations.
chapters.title: Phil Wong questions DOT on lighting under railroad trestles

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Councilmember Phil Wong.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yes, hi.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I would like to ask about the lighting specifically under trestles.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yes, I totally...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Support Councilmember Thomas Henry's issue on the dim areas on the 7th train on Roosevelt Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But I'm also concerned about the trestles like under the CSX or the Long Island Railroad tracks where clearly there's some inadequate illumination in my district and other places.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My question is, are those areas, can the DOT work on it to improve the illumination?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Or that's not city property and there's not much you could do about it.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you please talk about it?

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Yeah, no, as long as it's going over our right-of-way, we would have the ability to go take a look and see if we need to add lighting there, see if it's sufficient.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: So if you have locations, we can certainly evaluate.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yeah, because specifically as the lifespan of these fixtures, as they approach the end of life, they get dimmer.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And then when there's so many fixtures, they all get dim, either time to replace them or upgrade them, because that's an issue.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And quite often that leads to a lot of tracks, graffiti or crime and other issues.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So I'll work with your office on that.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But I support this bill.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks about the average annual consent fee range. Craven says fees range from a few hundred dollars to around $2,000-$2,500 on average, with some in central areas costing more, and clarifies this is the only annual fee businesses pay.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions DOT on Intro 918 fee ranges for businesses

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Thomas Henry, you may ask your questions on 918.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: On average, what is the range of the annual consent fee for businesses?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So off the top of my head, I'd say they range from a few hundred dollars.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: On average, it's probably $2,000, $2,500, something like that.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And then there are some in the central parts of the city and what's called Sector 4 in the legislation that would be in the thousands of dollars, like more thousands of dollars.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry probes DOT's concern about collecting fees after licensing. Craven explains the fee is paid at the end of the approval process, so refunds are not an issue, but DOT worries businesses may be less motivated to pay on time once already operating, requiring staff to chase payments.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions DOT on fee collection and refund concerns for Intro 918

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay, and as mentioned, this is just one of the fees that businesses have to pay when they're doing an outdoor dining?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: This is the only annual fee that they have to pay.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: There are several fees when you're setting up the program, when you first apply, and then from year to year, there is one annual fee.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay, so, and it was mentioned that if...

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Application is not, does not go through, just correct me if I'm wrong, does not go through, then the owner basically does not receive any of that couple of hundred to $2,500 back.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: That is not correct, actually.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: This fee is what you pay

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It's sort of the final fee before you get your license, and then it's the annual one.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So if it doesn't go through, you do not pay this fee.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So what was the concern around if the entity doesn't get the license, you guys will never see the fee?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: What's that concern?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: The concern is actually we have found various programs.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: that once someone has the authority to operate, and so you would pay the first fee, you would get your license.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Sometimes it is difficult.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We find that sometimes people are less interested in paying once they're kind of already out there, they have their license, they're like ready to go.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And then people don't always pay on time or they might be late and then we have to chase after people to get them to pay.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So as the structure is now, you pay the fee before you get your license?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Up front?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It's the last thing before you get your license, when you sign your revocable consent agreement.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: But if you do pay the fee and don't get your license, you get that back?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: You would, yeah, but that wouldn't happen.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: What do you mean that wouldn't happen?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: You've gone through the entire review process, the review and approval process, before you would pay this fee.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So, like, if you're paying this fee, we're good to go.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks about implementation costs and whether automatic quarterly payments could work. Craven says it would add staffing responsibilities or slow down approvals, and while automated billing exists, converting to quarterly automated invoicing would be a slow process.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions DOT on implementation cost and automated payment for Intro 918

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: How much does DOT anticipate it will cost to implement Introduction 918?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So I don't know if we've done a specific estimate, but we do believe it would add to our staffing responsibilities because it would be the same people who currently issue the invoices and also do things like register contracts.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Who are currently very busy full-time getting restaurants set up with their licenses.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So either we would have to add additional staff or it would have the effect of slowing down the approval process for other restaurants.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Have you considered an automatic payment process?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So once the person signs up,

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: They can automatically quarterly pay that fee?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We have automated billing for the annual fees currently.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: We would have to change it in our portal to get it to be a quarterly automated invoice process.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And that is something that is kind of a slow process to implement, unfortunately.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So definitely considered it.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It sounds great.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry argues quarterly payments would alleviate financial stress for small businesses, citing her tours of restaurants across the city where owners mention fees as a major burden. Craven acknowledges fees can be burdensome but says no restaurants have specifically requested quarterly payments, only advocates have.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry questions DOT on small business fee burden and quarterly payments

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Can we agree that oftentimes, especially for our small businesses, different fees can be cumbersome and burdensome, and this would alleviate some of that financial stress and obligation if we allow them to pay over a period of time versus up front?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So I'll agree with you.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Obviously, fees can definitely be burdensome for small businesses.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I think in this case, we actually haven't heard from any restaurants specifically requesting quarterly payments.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I've heard from the advocates, but not from any restaurants.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And we have found that restaurants are not very shy about asking us for things.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So if you are familiar with specific restaurants, we would certainly be interested in talking to you and getting more information.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So for the past two months, I've been doing tours of small businesses, predominantly restaurants across the city of New York.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And one of the main things they mention are the fees, specifically with outdoor dining cafes.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: A couple of restaurants have actually...

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Actually said that they've paid fees in the

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I'm not going to say it's this same fee, but they've paid fees in the past, have not been able to open their sidewalk cafe and had never received those fees back.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So this really derives from, again, just common sense that it helps to pay overtime versus one time and also actually speaking to businesses to see what would be helpful.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And when I discuss this with those businesses.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: They smile ear to ear and are super excited when I discuss it with businesses as well as community boards.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Because individuals who own businesses and individuals who know business owners recognize that we are in an affordability crisis, not just in housing, but across the board.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And our small businesses are getting hit very hard.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And this is just one measure we see as being able to take some of that financial burden off of them.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Do you have any more questions?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: You good for now?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Councilmember Marti, do you have questions?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: All right.

chapters.summary: Wong shares that constituent requests for burned-out street lamps take months to get a reply. Dean says replies should not take that long, explains repairs typically take 10 days for evaluation, and more complex wiring or Con Edison issues can take longer, but commits to improving communication.
chapters.title: Phil Wong questions DOT on street light repair response times

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Regarding intro, Councilmember Wong, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Regarding...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Replacement of fixtures in my district because I work for my predecessor, Robert Holden, and I handle constituent services.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: When we make a request to either repair or replace a burned out street lamp or like a fixture that's on a trestle, it takes a very long time just to get a reply.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Like I myself follow up on the case.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: It took three months for me to get a reply that, okay, we'll work on it.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: You know, can you talk about it?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Why does it take so long?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because constituents are concerned when you have, say, you live in a residential block and one street light out, you know, they feel very unsafe when that happens.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please talk about it.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Yeah, certainly we can.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Make sure we're getting a reply back sooner than that.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: It shouldn't take so long, certainly, to let you know that something's being worked on.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: As far as the repair itself, it can vary.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Typically, our contractors have 10 days to sort of evaluate

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: either make the repair or determine if something more substantial is necessary.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: So sometimes it's a fairly simple matter, like you said.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Sometimes it's just a burnt-out fixture that needs to be replaced.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: Other times there might be more extensive issues with the wiring or maybe with the con-ed feed.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: So in those instances, it can take longer to make the actual repair.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: But we can certainly make sure that we're at least keeping you informed on when those issues arise.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

chapters.summary: Abreu asks about anticipated costs for implementing Intro 894. Craven says there are no anticipated costs and explains DOT is reviewing rules for both sidewalk and roadway winterization to create consistent standards, including siding, wind protection, and snow load requirements.
chapters.title: Abreu questions DOT on Intro 894 implementation cost

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Regarding Intro 894, I know that you're mostly supportive, but I wanted to ask, how much does DOT anticipate it would cost to implement Intro 894?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I don't know that there are any anticipated costs.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I think we just say, you know, as I'm sure you know, we're having larger negotiations with the council about other outdoor dining bills, including winterization of roadway.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And something that we're doing right now is like we're taking a hard look at our rules to figure out what restaurants would need to do on the roadway to be able to winterize their setups without going into like a full scale shed situation like we had in the pandemic.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And I think we want to take a look at this for sidewalks as well and maybe treat the two similarly so we can have consistent standards for both.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Abreu asks what public notice DOT currently provides for disruptive work lasting at least seven days. Jeffrey Malamy describes DOT's notification toolkit including meetings with elected officials, community boards, flyers, street ambassadors at pedestrian hubs, and electronic sharing of project information.
chapters.title: Abreu questions DOT on Intro 866 current public notice practices

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Now I want to just pivot to my last set of questions on Intro 866 regarding Epstein's bill requiring DOT to post notification signs regarding upcoming disruptive work.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: This bill would require signage for work that would cause a disruption for at least seven days.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: What public notice does DOT currently provide for work that will cause disruption for at least seven days?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Are there issues in the process that could be fixed?

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: Hi, yes, thanks for that question.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: So I'm Jeffrey Malamy, Director of Street Improvement Projects.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: Happy to give you a response there.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: So currently there's quite a lot that the agency does to notify the public of projects, you know, when they're in the...

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: Like the project planning phase through meetings with elected officials and their staffs and community boards and other stakeholders that we're aware of.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: And those iterative processes likely lead to improving the performance of the projects that we're designing.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: Once we get closer to implementation, there's a lot more notification that we do.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: Again, through the elected officials offices and community boards, we post what's happening here flyers, which are kind of plain language, very easy to read, non-jargony,

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: that we put up along the corridor in advance of construction.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: We also share those electronically and appreciate.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: the work that you and your colleagues do to amplify those messages, to let even more of the community know that work will be ongoing.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: We also have a street ambassador program because we know not everyone has the time or the flexibility to be engaged in community board meetings or to attend workshops.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: So those folks often set up on the street by really popular pedestrian hubs and around

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: bus stops and subway stations and outside grocery stores so that New Yorkers just in the regular course of their days can happen by, learn about an upcoming project.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: They can talk with the street ambassadors who can answer their questions or if they're pressed for time, just pick up a pamphlet or a flyer and learn more about the project on their own time.

chapters.summary: Abreu asks how DOT defines disruptive work. Malamy says there is no defined standard and the bill's definition is quite broad, making signage requirements for minimal interruptions seem excessive. Abreu asks if DOT is open to conversations on defining triggers; Malamy agrees.
chapters.title: Abreu questions DOT on defining disruptive work under Intro 866

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: How does DOT define disruptive work generally?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Does it pertain to specific work being done, such as utility work, street resurfacing, and street reconstruction?

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: I think for, how do we define disruptive?

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: I think, I mean, it depends on the scope and scale of a project.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: All of those tools in our toolkit that I mentioned don't get deployed for every single project.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: But we have a good gauge working with your offices and your colleagues'offices and the community boards, understanding what the community would perceive as disruptive and then matching

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: the outreach materials to speak to that need.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: So there's no defined standard per se?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: For disruptive work?

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: No, no, not really.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: And I think, I mean, the way that it's defined in the bill is quite broad.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: And so kind of requiring signage to that extent for...

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: For the minimal interruption as described in the bill seems excessive to us and would get in the way of delivery.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: You're open to having conversations on defining that and what would trigger a notice?

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: Sure, yeah, definitely.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: Yeah, I think, I mean, just like our safety projects for an intersection or a corridor, it's not one size fits all.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: So it really depends on, you know, the makeup and the interests of the community and how best to notify members of ongoing work, the scope and scale of it.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: So we're happy to work with your offices to define that.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I'm sure the council members is up for working with you guys on that.

chapters.summary: Abreu asks what role contractors play in posting notification signs and about implementation costs. Malamy says contractors already have statutory notice requirements, and while he lacks a specific budget figure, additional signage work would detract from other outreach efforts DOT finds most effective.
chapters.title: Abreu questions DOT on contractor roles and implementation cost for Intro 866

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: What role do contractors or utilities play in ensuring that these notification signs are put up?

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: Yeah, so certainly, you know, we have a number of notice requirements in statutes and in rules that, you know, non-DOT partners like contractors

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: are required to install along the corridor of a project or of their work.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: How much does DOT anticipate it would cost to implement Intro A66?

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: I don't have a budget amount, but we are sensitive to the fact that

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: additional signage work with printing and installing and maintaining and updating and removing those signs so there isn't

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
