utterances.text: litter on the streets would detract from the other work that the agency does as far as

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: coordinating with elected officials'offices and holding the meetings and street ambassador deployments, as I mentioned, that we feel are

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: most effective at getting the word out and informing the public and hearing back from the public on their concerns around a given project.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: So we're sensitive to those tradeoffs.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Councillor Amarte?

chapters.summary: Councilmember Marte asks about replicating winterization standards between sidewalk and roadway cafes. Craven explains both currently must be open-air with some coverings, and DOT is exploring temporary siding options for wind and snow protection with consistent standards for both setups.
chapters.title: Marte questions DOT on Intro 894 winterization consistency between sidewalk and roadway cafes

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you for being here.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I just wanted to clarify one of the points that you made.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: You said that in regards to Brewer's Bill 894 and the winterization of Sidewalk Cafe, you mentioned that you wanted something that could be replicated on the roadside.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Can you talk about that a little bit further?

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: How would that look like?

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: What are some potential options of something that could be replicated from the sidewalk to a roadside cafe?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I think we're still kind of in early discussions about this, so I don't want to get too specific into anything.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: But I think both roadway and sidewalk currently are required to be open air, right?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So you can have some sort of coverings on both, but you're not really supposed to have anything on the side.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So we're trying to figure out what kind of siding could be put up on a temporary basis through the winter.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Ideally with the dates to match on both, which they currently don't quite do with this bill, but to have something that could provide some kind of wind protection and snow protection and whatever else on the sides of the

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: setups for the comfort of everyone sitting inside.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: But I think we would want some kind of similar materials, similar requirements, protection from the snow.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: There might be standards to make sure that the roof doesn't collapse if there's too much snow, something like that.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So those kinds of things are what we're trying to work out.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And we think it would probably make sense to look at those things together and just be consistent.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It'll be easier for us.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It'll be easier for the restaurants.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It'll be easier for everyone sort of trying to.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Marte raises concerns about establishments violating SLA stipulations regarding outdoor dining, such as serving liquor when not permitted. Craven explains SLA licenses are outside DOT's jurisdiction as a state entity, and DOT cannot enforce stipulations that conflict with city legislation or rules.
chapters.title: Marte questions DOT on SLA enforcement at outdoor dining establishments

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And I know we've talked to DOT a number of times about this, but the SLA has stipulations that they make with the community board on, like, whether they have windows opens, close, hours of serving drinks.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: However, a lot of times, and we've done walkthrough in my district, and I know Councilmember Epstein has done walkthrough in his district, where we see people take advantage of, you know, whether it's sidewalk or the roadside dining, to continue to serve liquor when the SLA,

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: you know, says they can't, or whether they made an agreement with the community board that they shouldn't.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: What can DOT do to make sure that the agreements that are made in the community board or with the SLA are actually monitored and enforced on both the sidewalk and roadside?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: So that's a great question.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It's kind of a tricky question because the SLA requirements are outside of the SLA licenses are outside of our jurisdiction, right?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: It's a state entity.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Like we don't have the authorization to enforce that.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And so I have a quick follow up.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: So.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Because they do a lot of stipulations with the community board during the SLA process of how an establishment is going to function and look like,

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: would DOT be able to respect or promote or advocate to the owner of that establishment to do stuff in accordance to those agreements that have been made in the past?

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: I think it would depend at a minimum on what those requirements were, what those stipulations were, because I know there's certain language in the legislation that prevents us from enforcing certain things like hours of operation.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And those agreements, some of those stipulations I understand are things like hours of operation, and they wouldn't necessarily...

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: align.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: And so anything that conflicts with the legislation or the rules, we would not be able to enforce.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about street ambassadors, noting he has never seen one in his district during major projects. Malamy explains the team of 12-15 ambassadors is deployed project-by-project across all five boroughs, coordinated by project managers and borough commissioners, and offers to follow up on specific district projects.
chapters.title: Phil Wong questions DOT on street ambassador program visibility

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Councillor Phil Wong.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yes, you were mentioning about street ambassadors.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you talk about how often they come visited?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because I've never seen mine.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: If you can tell me who's the street ambassador in my district, I would certainly like to reach out to him or her.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: Yeah, happy to talk about that program.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: So they serve all five boroughs, and it's a team of like 12 to 15.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: And so they're deployed kind of throughout the city on a project-by-project basis.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: Between our DOT project managers and the borough commissioners'offices, strategize about when and where.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: To deploy the street ambassadors, whether they're gathering feedback from the public or explaining a project that's soon to be implemented.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: So I'm not sure, like I could get back to you on projects in your district.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I would certainly need the ambassador around when there are major projects in my district.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And there were several of them and I haven't seen any ambassadors like collecting feedback.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I had to do that.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: All right, so I welcome the ambassador of my district at the next project.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jeffrey Malamy
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Joe.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilor Wong.

chapters.summary: Abreu asks how many of the 2,500 lighting upgrades in 2025 were under elevated train lines. Dean says he will need to check the breakdown and follow up. Abreu then adjourns the administration portion of the hearing.
chapters.title: Abreu questions DOT on lighting upgrades under elevated train lines

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I want to go back quick to intro 845.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: In your testimony, you said that DOT upgraded lighting at more than 2,500 locations across the city in 2025.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: How many?

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Many of those locations were under elevated train lines.

utterances.speaker: Monty Dean
utterances.text: We'll have to get back to you and check on what the breakdown is of that.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Please do.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I have no other questions to ask.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: So this hearing is hereby adjourned.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Sorry, not this hearing, this portion of the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: You're excused.

chapters.summary: Abreu opens the floor for public testimony, reminding attendees of formal proceeding rules, the two-minute speaking limit, and procedures for submitting written testimony by email within 72 hours.
chapters.title: Chair Abreu opens public testimony and gives instructions

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Sorry, I now open the hearing for public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: I remind members of the public that this is a formal government proceeding and that the quorum shall be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: As such, members of the public shall remain silent at all times.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: The witness table is reserved for people who wish to testify.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: No video recording or photography is allowed from the witness table.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Further, members of the public may not present audio or video recordings of testimony, but may submit transcripts of such recordings to the sergeant at arms for inclusion in the hearing record.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: If you wish to speak at today's hearing, please fill out an appearance card with the sergeant at arms and wait to be recognized.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: When recognized, you have two minutes to speak on the legislation being heard today.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: If you have a written statement or additional written testimony you wish to submit for the record, please provide a copy of that testimony to the sergeant at arms.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: You may also email written testimony to testimony at council.nyc.gov within 72 hours of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Audio and video recordings will not be accepted.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Now I'm going to call the first panel, Andrew Ridgy and Vinny Dons.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And Andrew, you can start whenever you're ready.

chapters.summary: Andrew Rigie of the NYC Hospitality Alliance supports Intro 894, arguing restaurants pay year-round for sidewalk cafe space but cannot use it in winter. He says winter vestibules would help small businesses maximize revenue and sustain worker hours during cold months.
chapters.title: Andrew Rigie testifies on Intro 894 seasonal enclosures for sidewalk cafes

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: My name is Andrew Ridgy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: I am the executive director of the New York City Hospitality Alliance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: We represent restaurants, bars, and nightclubs across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Thank you for hosting today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: I'm going to testify on three bills, Intro 894, 917, and 918.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: I've also submitted written testimony.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: First on 894, seasonal enclosures for sidewalk cafes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Restaurants are paying the city for a year-round license for their sidewalk cafes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: But as mentioned earlier, during the more inclement months, they're unable to really utilize that space, even though they're paying for it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: As Councilmember Brewer mentioned, we would like the ability to use high-quality winter vestibules.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: You have seen restaurants that have used them in the past.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: They hold up to the elements.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: They allow businesses, small businesses in particular, to really maximize their revenue by using the space that they're paying for throughout the winter months.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: It's also important worker protection as when the weather gets cooler, you see restaurants.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Restaurants are reducing worker hours, even eliminating some jobs because they're losing that space.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: If they're able to utilize it by keeping it warm during the winter, then they will be able to sustain those jobs as well as sustain the small business revenue and giving their customers a place to sit.

chapters.summary: Rigie supports allowing quarterly fee payments, noting this was standard practice at DCWP for decades. He argues the city should not require lump-sum payments when even landlords don't demand a full year's rent upfront, and that total setup costs can reach tens of thousands of dollars.
chapters.title: Andrew Rigie testifies on Intro 918 quarterly fee payments

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: The next bill we are speaking on is the 918, which would allow the revocable consent fees to be paid quarterly instead of in one lump sum.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: This is how it was done for probably decades at DCWP.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Not even a small business's landlord requires one year rent paid up front.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Why is the city requiring small businesses to pay fees in a lump sum?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: It's not just the consent fees.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: It's also the licensing fee, security deposit, the fee for the public notice, you know, architect's fees, if they're paying that, expediter fees.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: It is a lot of money.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: You could be talking tens of thousands of dollars.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: It worked in the past, paying quarterly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: We think it would be a nice option, especially when we talk about supporting small businesses in neighborhoods across the city.

chapters.summary: Rigie acknowledges existing DOT rules already address cleanliness but asks that if penalties are codified, first-time violations allow a warning or cure period before monetary fines, consistent with broader small business reform efforts and Executive Order 11.
chapters.title: Andrew Rigie testifies on Intro 917 cleanliness standards and penalty concerns

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: And can I get 30 seconds to finish up the last one?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Obviously, we all want to keep the streets, you know, clean, the outdoor dining clean.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: We believe, as DOT acknowledged and was mentioned earlier, that the rules currently establish a regulatory framework and penalties if the

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: sidewalk or roadway cafe area is not kept clean.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: We don't necessarily love doubling up the fine scheme.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: However, that being said, if you are going to codify what's already in the rules, we would just ask that violations, or I should say first-time violations, allow for a warning or a cure period before a monetary penalty is issued.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: The council I know is working on small business reforms.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: The mayor's administration through executive order 11 is looking at reducing fines and fees.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: And we believe as the hospitality lines, any small business violation that does not pose an immediate hazard to workers or to the public should allow a warning or cure period before a penalty is issued.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: We think the focus should be on education training first, penalties as a last resort.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Andrew Rigie
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Vinny.

chapters.summary: High school student Vinny Dong testifies in support of Intros 845 and 866, describing how students travel across the city at early and late hours and face unsafe conditions when streets are dark under elevated trains, especially in winter when daylight is limited.
chapters.title: Vinny Dong testifies on student safety and lighting under elevated trains

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: Thank you for allowing me to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: My name is Vinny Dong, and I'm a current sophomore at Queens High School for the Sciences.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: As a student, I am here to testify about Intro 845-2026 and Intro 866-2026.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: As a student, we are across all five boroughs of New York City, and we travel all across the city to get to our schools every single day, Monday through Friday, at sometimes absurd hours.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: Sometimes we wake up at 6 o'clock and sometimes we get home at 9 o'clock because of extracurriculars, getting to school, the distance.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: It is essential for the Department of Transportation and this committee to invest on the infrastructure surrounding the trains, on the passes and streets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: When winter comes and the daylight time shrinks, sometimes it gets dark at around 6 or 7.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: When streets get dark, stuff happens.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: It's well reported in analytics, it's well reported in crime reports, and when streets are unsafe,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: Parents cannot assure that their child will come home safe either.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: No student should have to give up their access to extracurricular activities or DYCD programs or any other programs they do outside of school simply because transportation is unsafe or uncertain.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: Additionally, I would like to branch out this issue more than infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: Sometimes when transportation is delayed or uncertain or when the roads are uneven, it poses a significant threat to students, especially those with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Vinny Dong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Michelle Craven
utterances.text: Councilman Marte?

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I just want to say thank you for coming today and for testifying.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I think what you said is exactly what this legislation is meant to achieve.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: And so I just want to thank you for having the courage, showing up to a city council hearing and saying how you and other students feel.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I was excused.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If we inadvertently missed anyone who registered to testify today and is yet to be called, please use the Zoom raise hand function if you are testifying remotely, and you will be called in the order that your hand has been raised.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you are testifying in person, please come to the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I will need you both to fill out an appearance card.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: They're bringing an appearance card for you to fill out, and then you'll be able to testify.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Give us a moment.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Blake Walker.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please turn on your mic.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You're fine.

chapters.summary: Park Slope resident Blake Walker describes a bike loop connecting Prospect Park to Coney Island and Bay Ridge, arguing that missing signage prevents tourists and many locals from discovering the route. He proposes adding signage to capitalize on bicycle tourism and spread economic benefits across Brooklyn.
chapters.title: Blake Walker testifies on bike path signage and connectivity in Brooklyn

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: Hi, my name is Blake Walker.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: I live in Park Slope, Brooklyn, and I'd like to speak about a different infrastructure issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: So at the start of each summer, my dad and I ride our bikes from Prospect Park to Coney Island along the Ocean Parkway bike path.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: And embarking on a journey from a 585-acre urban park to the sea is an experience that's unique to New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: But the problem is that there's a lot of missed potential to extend this opportunity to the 10 million people who visit Prospect Park annually, as many

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: locals and virtually all tourists are actually unaware of the opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: So on this year's bike ride, my dad and I decided to further our little biking exploration.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: By figuring out a way to connect Coney Island to the Shore Parkway bike path.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: So we rode along the waterfront at Coney Island to Owl's Head and Bay Ridge

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: before taking the New York City Greenway bike path to another protected path along 4th Avenue, then turning onto 15th Street before returning to Prospect Park, which effectively created a loop.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: And the loop was an obvious missed opportunity for New York City to capitalize on bicycle to

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: And the current problem is that this loop that I just spoke of, which connects a lot of historic locations and beautiful landscapes, is disconnected.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: And the infrastructure is in place, but the...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: The signage is not like there's no signage on the bike paths to effectively guide riders.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: And although some locals might be able to figure it out, a lot of tourists are just not going to even try.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: So simply closing the loop.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: By adding signage and calling it something like the Prospect Park, Coney Island, Bay Ridge Loop, would allow Brooklyn to capitalize on the $150 billion

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: a year in bicycle tourism that's generated.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: It would also, sorry, have one second.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: And it would also enable all of Brooklyn to benefit from the tourism, which often stay centralized and affluent neighborhoods currently.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Blake.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I really appreciate you coming to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Blake Walker
utterances.text: And thanks for having me.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Of course, always.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Always welcome.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: I'm just going to do another check to see if there's anyone on Zoom who would like to testify.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please use the Zoom raise hand function if you're testifying remotely.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And if you're in person, please come to the dais.

chapters.summary: Council staff confirms no further witnesses wish to testify and officially adjourns the hearing.
chapters.title: Hearing adjourned

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Seeing none, I will now close the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you to the members of the administration and the members of the public who have joined us today.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: This hearing is officially adjourned.

summary: Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure hearing on five bills: Int 845 (lighting under elevated trains), Int 866 (disruptive work signage), Int 894 (seasonal sidewalk cafe enclosures), Int 917 (cafe cleanliness standards), and Int 918 (quarterly cafe fee payments).

DOT Deputy Commissioner Monty Dean and staff expressed support for some bills but raised concerns about Intro 845 limiting DOT to distinctive lighting, Intro 866 duplicating existing notice practices, and Intro 918 creating collection challenges.

Public testimony included support from the NYC Hospitality Alliance for seasonal enclosures and quarterly fees, a high school student advocating for lighting and safety, and a resident proposing bike path signage improvements.
title: Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure
